text: The surgeon uses a tips-up grasper to place a purse string suture with zero silk above the expected fluid level in the colon, preparing to decompress the cecum and prevent fluid spillage.

The surgeon docks four robotic arms to facilitate the decompressive step. Using curved monopolar scissors, an enterotomy is created, while a suction irrigator is positioned in the fourth arm for immediate use.
The surgeon identifies the anatomy of the transverse colon distal to the cecal volvulus, with the appendix clearly visible after decompression of the colon.

The surgeon identifies landmarks proximal and distal to the volvulus and begins transecting the mesocolon. The mesentery is transected immediately under the intestine to preserve perfusion for the anastomosis, minimizing the risk of bleeding.

The surgeon rotates the bowel to ensure complete transection of the mesentery from the involved intestine, using a vessel sealer to separate the pathologic section of the colon from the surgical field, gradually improving exposure despite the bulky cecum.

The surgeon completes the transection of the mesentery before injecting ICG dye. The fluorescent camera is then used to evaluate the perfusion at the distal and proximal staple lines, confirming adequate blood flow to the bowel.

The surgeon removes the disconnected pathologic bowel from the surgical field, proceeding with a simple ileocolic anastomosis. Hemorrhagic ascites are visible, which were noted at the beginning of the operation due to the acute volvulus.

The surgeon aspirates the surgical field before performing the ileocolic anastomosis, anticipating exposure to pressurized small bowel and colonic contents. Enterotomies are created with stay sutures held upright to prevent passive flow of enteric contents into the abdomen. The anastomosis is completed using a standard two-layer closure technique.
The surgeon uses a 2-0 Vicryl suture to close the mucosal layer and a 3-0 PDS suture to imbricate the suture line during the anastomosis procedure.
